title: When Transfer Learning Works Best - and When It Doesn't
concept: Transfer learning is strongest when the pretrained source images and the new target task are fairly similar, while large domain gaps — such as moving from natural photos to medical or satellite imagery — can weaken how much pretrained CNN features help.

exercises.prompt: Recap: why might a team fine-tune a pretrained CNN instead of training only a new head?

options.text: Because the backbone features themselves may need to adapt to the new task
Because fine-tuning removes the need for images
Because a frozen backbone cannot extract features

exercises.explain: Right — and how much adaptation you need depends a lot on how similar the old and new image worlds really are.

cards.title: Transferred skills work best between related jobs
cards.body: A chef switching restaurants still benefits from knife skills. The same chef switching to airplane repair gets much less direct help. Pretrained CNNs behave similarly: reuse is strongest when the new visual task resembles the old one in patterns, textures, objects, and image statistics.

cards.title: What a domain gap means
cards.body: A domain gap is the difference between the source data used in pretraining and the target data in your new task. Small domain gaps — for example, natural photos to other natural-photo tasks — usually transfer well. Large gaps, such as natural photos to X-rays, pathology slides, or satellite imagery, can weaken reuse because the visual world has changed a lot.

cards.title: Road bike to road race vs. road bike to ski slope
cards.body: If you already know how to ride a road bike, a different road race feels familiar. A snowy ski slope does not. Transfer learning is similar: related terrains preserve more of what you learned, while very different terrains force bigger adaptation.

cards.title: Natural photos transfer farther than you might think — but not infinitely
cards.body: ImageNet-style pretraining often helps with products, animals, street scenes, or other everyday photos because the low- and mid-level patterns overlap a lot. But if you move to chest X-rays or satellite crops, the textures, viewpoints, and semantics can differ enough that the head start becomes weaker and deeper fine-tuning or domain-specific pretraining may matter more.

cards.title: A big domain gap does not mean pretraining is useless
cards.body: A common myth is that once the new domain looks different, transfer learning is pointless. Not true. Early CNN layers may still contribute edges, blobs, and generic structures. The real lesson is not 'never reuse' but 'expect smaller gains and more adaptation when the domain gap is large.'

cards.title: Small gap vs. large gap
cards.body: Similarity changes how much of the pretrained CNN transfers cleanly.

left.text:
Natural photos -> similar natural photos
Features often transfer smoothly
Frozen backbones may already work well

right.text:
Natural photos -> medical or satellite imagery
Reuse may weaken or need more fine-tuning
Sometimes domain-specific pretraining helps more

exercises.prompt: Which target task is most likely to benefit smoothly from an ImageNet-pretrained CNN?

options.text: Analyzing chest X-rays with very different image structure
Predicting stock prices from text-only reports

exercises.explain: Correct — product photos live much closer to natural-photo pretraining than medical scans do.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A large domain gap always means a pretrained CNN is completely useless.
exercises.explain: False — the gains may shrink, but some reusable low-level structure can still remain.

exercises.sentence: Transfer learning usually works best when the source and target domains are more ___.
exercises.explain: Exactly — related image worlds make reuse easier.

exercises.prompt: Which statement shows the best understanding of when transfer learning can struggle?

options.text: It can weaken when the new images come from a very different domain, such as moving from everyday photos to medical or satellite imagery
It fails only because pretrained models dislike new class names
It works equally well for every image type, so domain mismatch never matters

exercises.explain: Yes — the key issue is similarity of visual structure, not just similarity of label words.

exercises.prompt: A team wants to adapt an ImageNet-pretrained CNN to classify satellite images. Which expectation is most realistic?

options.text: Pretraining may still help, especially in early visual features, but the large domain gap means the gains may be weaker and deeper adaptation could be needed
Transfer learning will behave exactly the same as it would on ordinary natural-photo classification
The model must keep its original ImageNet head because new domains cannot use new heads
Satellite images are not images, so CNN transfer learning is automatically impossible

exercises.explain: Exactly. Transfer learning is strongest when the source and target image worlds overlap, and it becomes less automatic when the domain gap grows. That practical judgment sets up the final capstone: choosing and reusing CNN architectures as a coherent vision toolkit.
